name: Bolivian Scrambled Eggs with Llajwa
steps: Beat the eggs and scramble gently in the butter until just set.
Meanwhile, mix the tomatoes and jalapeno with a pinch of salt for a quick llajwa-style salsa.
Serve the eggs topped with the salsa.
steps_detailed: Beat the eggs with a pinch of salt and scramble gently in the butter over low heat until just set and creamy.
Combine the diced tomatoes, jalapeno, aji amarillo paste, and half the cilantro for a quick llajwa-style salsa.
Plate the eggs and spoon the salsa over the top, finishing with the remaining cilantro.

name: Bolivian Pan de Queso
steps: Mix the cassava starch, cheese, egg, and melted butter into a soft dough.
Shape into small rolls.
Bake at 400F for 15-18 minutes until golden and puffed.
steps_detailed: Combine the cassava starch and crumbled queso fresco in a bowl.
Add the egg, melted butter, and milk, and mix until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms.
Shape into small rolls about 2 inches across and place on a baking sheet.
Bake at 400F (200C) for 15-20 minutes until puffed and golden.
Best eaten warm, straight from the oven.

name: Bolivian Api con Pastel
steps: Whisk the cornmeal into the milk with the sugar and cinnamon.
Simmer gently, stirring often, for 10 minutes until thickened into a warm, drinkable porridge.
Serve hot in a mug, traditionally alongside a small piece of fried pastry.
steps_detailed: Whisk the cornmeal into about 1/4 cup cold water first to form a smooth slurry with no lumps.
Warm the milk with the sugar, cinnamon, and anise seed in a saucepan.
Whisk in the cornmeal slurry and simmer gently over low heat, stirring frequently, for 10-12 minutes until thickened to a smooth, pourable porridge - it should coat the back of a spoon.
Serve hot in a mug. Traditional api is made with purple corn flour for its deep color, but this version keeps the same warm, spiced flavor.

name: Bolivian Silpancho
steps: Bread and fry the pounded chicken until golden and cooked through (or simply pan-fry if you skip the breading).
Fry the potato slices until golden, and fry the egg to your liking.
Serve the chicken over the rice, topped with the fried potato and egg.
steps_detailed: Pound the chicken breast thin and season with salt.
Dredge lightly in flour and fry in the oil over medium-high heat, 2-3 minutes per side, until golden and cooked through.
In the same pan, fry the potato slices until golden and crisp, then fry the egg to your liking (a runny yolk is traditional).
Toss the diced tomatoes and onion together for a quick fresh topping.
Plate the rice, top with the fried chicken, then the potatoes, the fried egg, and finish with the tomato-onion topping.

name: Bolivian Fricase Paceno
steps: Brown the pork with the onion and aji amarillo paste.
Add 1.5 cups water and the hominy, and simmer 30-35 minutes until the pork is tender.
Season and serve hot with extra broth.
steps_detailed: Brown the pork shoulder with the onion, garlic, and aji amarillo paste over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add 2 cups water, the hominy, bay leaf, and oregano. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and simmer 30-35 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the pork is very tender and the broth has a good spicy depth.
Remove the bay leaf, stir in the cilantro, and serve hot in bowls with plenty of the broth.

name: Bolivian Sopa de Mani

ingredients.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Simmer the chicken with the carrot in 2 cups water for 20 minutes; shred the meat and return to the pot.
Whisk in the peanut butter until smooth, then add the rice and simmer 15 minutes until tender.
Season and serve hot.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken thigh with the onion, carrot, bell pepper, and chicken broth plus 1 cup water for 20-25 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Remove the chicken, shred it, and return it to the pot.
Whisk in the peanut butter a spoonful at a time until fully dissolved into the broth.
Add the rice and simmer 15-18 minutes until tender, stirring occasionally so the peanut doesn't stick to the bottom.
Stir in the cilantro and serve hot.

name: Bolivian Majadito
steps: Brown the beef and season well; simmer with the diced tomatoes for 5 minutes.
Cook the rice separately, then stir it into the beef mixture.
Top with the sliced hard-boiled egg.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground beef with the onion, garlic, and cumin over medium-high heat, 6-8 minutes.
Stir in the diced tomatoes and simmer 5-6 minutes to let the flavors meld.
Meanwhile, cook the rice separately in water until tender, then drain.
Stir the cooked rice into the beef mixture until well combined and heated through.
Top with the sliced hard-boiled egg and fried plantain slices before serving.

name: Bolivian Pique a lo Macho

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Sear the sliced beef over high heat until well browned.
Fry the potato wedges until golden and crisp.
Toss the beef and potatoes with the tomato and onion, and serve together on one plate.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Season the sliced beef with salt and sear it in a hot pan with a little of the oil, 2-3 minutes, until well browned but still tender. Set aside.
Fry the potato wedges in the remaining oil until golden and crisp, 12-15 minutes, turning occasionally.
In the same pan, quickly saute the onion and jalapeno until just softened.
Combine the beef, potatoes, sauteed onion and jalapeno, and diced tomatoes on a large plate.
Top with the sliced hard-boiled egg and serve immediately, family-style.

name: Bolivian Chairo

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Simmer the beef in water 30 minutes until nearly tender.
Add the potato and carrot and simmer 15 minutes.
Stir in the cooked quinoa and simmer 5 more minutes, then season and serve hot.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Combine the beef, onion, bay leaf, and 2.5 cups water in a pot. Simmer 30-35 minutes until the beef is tender.
Add the potato and carrot, and simmer 15 minutes.
Add the green beans and cooked quinoa, and simmer 5-8 more minutes until everything is tender and heated through.
Remove the bay leaf, stir in the cilantro, and serve hot in bowls with the broth.

name: Bolivian Picante de Pollo
steps: Brown the chicken thigh with the onion and aji amarillo paste.
Add a cup of water and simmer covered 25 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and tender.
Serve with the boiled potato alongside.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken thigh with the onion, garlic, and aji amarillo paste over medium-high heat, 6-8 minutes.
Add the chicken broth, bring to a simmer, then cover and cook over low heat 25-30 minutes until the chicken is tender and cooked through.
Meanwhile, boil the potato chunks in a separate pot until tender.
Stir the cilantro into the chicken stew and serve with the boiled potato alongside.

name: Bolivian Charque de Res

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Season the thin-sliced beef well with salt and let it sit 10 minutes.
Fry in the oil over medium-high heat until well browned and slightly crisp at the edges.
Serve with the corn and boiled potato alongside, in the traditional style of dried, shredded beef.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Season the thin-sliced beef generously with salt and the minced garlic, mimicking the concentrated flavor of dried charque.
Fry in the oil over medium-high heat, 2-3 minutes per side, until well browned and crisp at the edges.
Boil the corn and potato separately until tender.
Plate the beef with the corn and potato alongside, topped with the sliced hard-boiled egg.

name: Bolivian Saltenas
steps: Simmer the chicken and potato with the aji amarillo paste and a splash of water until the chicken is cooked and the mixture is saucy and slightly thickened; chill until firm.
Spoon the chilled filling into the dough, fold into a pocket, and crimp a rope-like seal along the edge.
Bake at 375F for 20-25 minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, then add the chicken, potato, aji amarillo paste, and brown sugar with a splash of water. Simmer 15-18 minutes until the chicken is cooked and the mixture is thick and saucy - saltenas are known for their juicy filling, so don't let it dry out completely.
Stir in the chopped egg and olives, then chill the filling thoroughly (at least an hour) so it firms up enough to handle.
Make a simple enriched dough with the flour, butter, a pinch of salt, and warm water, kneaded smooth and rested 20 minutes.
Roll into rounds, spoon the chilled filling onto each, fold into a half-moon pocket, and crimp a decorative rope-twist seal along the curved edge to seal in the juices.
Bake at 375F (190C) for 22-26 minutes until golden brown. Let cool slightly before eating - the filling stays hot and juicy inside.

name: Bolivian Anticuchos

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Toss the beef cubes with the aji amarillo paste, garlic, and oil, and let marinate 20 minutes if you have time.
Thread onto skewers and grill or pan-sear until well charred outside and cooked to your liking.
Serve hot off the skewer.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Combine the aji amarillo paste, garlic, oil, vinegar, and cumin, and toss the beef cubes in the marinade.
Let marinate at least 30 minutes, or up to a few hours in the fridge for deeper flavor.
Thread the beef onto skewers and grill over high heat, or sear in a very hot pan, 2-3 minutes per side, until well charred outside.
Serve immediately, hot off the skewer.

name: Bolivian Empanadas de Queso
steps: Fill each tortilla with the crumbled cheese, fold, and press the edges to seal.
Fry 2 minutes per side until golden and the cheese has melted.
Drain briefly before serving hot.

ingredients_full.note: for a hint of sweetness in the dough

steps_detailed: Combine the queso fresco and cotija in a bowl.
Spoon the cheese mixture onto one half of each tortilla, fold over, and press the edges firmly to seal, crimping with a fork if needed.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the filled turnovers 2-3 minutes per side until golden and the cheese inside is melted.
Drain on a paper towel and serve immediately.

name: Bolivian Tucumanas
steps: Simmer the chicken and potato with the aji amarillo paste and a splash of water until cooked and thickened; let cool.
Fill the dough with the cooled mixture, fold into a half-moon, and seal well.
Fry in the oil until deep golden on both sides, about 3-4 minutes per side - unlike baked saltenas, these are always fried.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, then add the chicken, potato, and aji amarillo paste with a splash of water. Simmer 15-18 minutes until the chicken is cooked and the mixture is thick, not soupy.
Stir in the chopped egg and let the filling cool completely, then chill briefly so it's easy to handle.
Make a simple dough with the flour, butter, a pinch of salt, and warm water, kneaded smooth and rested 15 minutes.
Roll into rounds, fill with the cooled mixture, fold into a half-moon, and crimp the edges firmly to seal.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the tucumanas 3-4 minutes per side until deep golden brown and crisp.
Drain on a paper towel and serve hot.

name: Bolivian Bunuelos
steps: Mix the flour, egg, a pinch of salt, and enough milk or water to form a soft, sticky dough.
Drop spoonfuls into the hot oil and fry until puffed and golden, turning to cook evenly.
Warm the brown sugar with a splash of water into a light syrup and drizzle over the fried dough.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, egg, a pinch of salt, and enough water to form a soft, slightly sticky dough. Rest 10 minutes.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat. Drop spoonfuls of the dough into the hot oil and fry, turning occasionally, until puffed, golden, and cooked through, about 2-3 minutes per side.
Meanwhile, warm the brown sugar with the cinnamon, anise seed, and a splash of water into a light syrup.
Drain the fried dough briefly and drizzle generously with the warm syrup before serving.

name: Bolivian Fruit Salad

ingredients.note: diced - yes, savory-sweet fruit salads often include avocado

steps: Combine your favorite chopped tropical fruit (papaya, banana, orange) with the papaya nectar as a light syrup.
Toss in the diced avocado and lime juice.
Chill briefly before serving.
steps_detailed: Combine chopped tropical fruit of your choice (papaya, banana, orange segments, or pineapple all work well) in a bowl.
Add the papaya nectar and brown sugar, stirring gently to coat the fruit in a light, sweet syrup.
Fold in the diced avocado and lime juice just before serving, keeping it gentle so the fruit doesn't break down.
Chill 10-15 minutes before serving.

name: Bolivian Postre de Quinoa
steps: Simmer the cooked quinoa with the milk, sugar, and cinnamon for 8-10 minutes until it thickens into a soft pudding.
Serve warm or chilled.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Combine the cooked quinoa, milk, brown sugar, and cinnamon in a small saucepan.
Simmer over low heat, stirring often, for 10-12 minutes until the mixture thickens to a soft, creamy pudding consistency.
Stir in the raisins during the last few minutes of cooking so they plump slightly.
Serve warm, or chill for a firmer, cooler dessert - both are common ways to enjoy it.

name: Bolivian Llajwa
steps: Combine the tomatoes, jalapeno, cilantro, and onion in a bowl.
Mash together roughly with a fork so it's chunky but well combined.
Season with salt and serve alongside almost anything.
steps_detailed: Combine the tomatoes, jalapeno, cilantro, onion, and garlic in a bowl.
Mash everything together roughly with a fork or the back of a spoon - traditionally done in a stone mortar - until well combined but still a bit chunky.
Stir in the lime juice and season generously with salt.
Serve as the everyday table sauce alongside almost any Bolivian dish.

name: Bolivian Salsa de Mani

ingredients.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Whisk the peanut butter with the warm broth until smooth.
Stir in the jalapeno and season with salt.
Serve warm over potatoes or grilled meat.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Warm the chicken broth gently in a small saucepan.
Whisk in the peanut butter a little at a time until fully smooth and pourable.
Stir in the jalapeno and garlic, and simmer 2-3 minutes to soften their bite.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving warm over boiled potatoes or grilled meats.
